"Leave If You Can": A Live Conversation with Amelia Frank-Vitale on Her New Book about Life in Honduras After Deportation
events.description: The enduring power of the idea of deportation can be explained, in part, by the mythology surrounding what deportation can and cannot do as a tool of social control. For most Americans, the world starts and ends at the border. The border—itself typically left undefined—acts as an invisible epistemic force field through which migrants suddenly appear (in moments of spectacular, unexplained arrival) and disappear (through the magic spell of deportation). events.title: Faig Ahmed: Textiles of Consciousness
events.description: Faig Ahmed’s work is a contemporary take on the textile traditions of his native Azerbaijan. Beginning from the understanding that textiles join families and societies through their patterned stories, Ahmed’s practice experiments with the role of carpets in Azerbaijani social and cultural history. The pixelated aesthetics that emerge in his large-scale works confound our cognitive and sensory expectations of craft-based textiles. On the Rapport Between Painting and Photography in Pop Art
events.description: For the great Pop artist Richard Hamilton, photography was implicated in modern painting from the beginning (Courbet, Manet, Degas…), and fundamental to his art is the exploration of this continued implication. Over his long career, Hamilton worked through several photographic formats as either source or structure of his pictures: the magazine advertisement, the publicity still, the fashion shoot, the postcard. events.description: Heteroresistance is a form of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) where subpopulations of seemingly isogenic bacterial cultures can grow during an antibiotic treatment that inhibits the growth of the majority; furthermore, that ability to grow can either be inherited by the subpopulation progeny (stable heteroresistance) or quickly lost (transient/unstable heteroresistance) after antibiotic treatment ends. events.title: Molecular Dynamics Simulations of Plasma-Surface Interactions for Semiconductor Device Manufacturing
events.description: Understanding the fundamental mechanisms of plasma-surface interactions is of particular interest for device manufacturing in the semiconductor industry. While classical interatomic potentials for molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are available for various plasma-surface systems, their development can be challenging for complex combinations of multiple atom types. Machine learning (ML) techniques can be utilized to develop flexible models trained on data from quantum chemical calculations, providing ab initio levels of accuracy at a lower computational cost. events.title: Simulations of Plasma Interactions with Diamond Surfaces
events.description: Diamond, a superlative material in many respects, is host to nitrogen vacancy (NV) center defects. NV centers have enabled many high-technology quantum devices for both quantum sensing and quantum computing thanks to their unique magnetic and optical properties. Many of these applications require NV centers to be close to the diamond surface, but surface defects can easily introduce electromagnetic noise and charge instability, hindering performance. Diamond surfaces therefore are engineered to be as defect-free, smooth, and appropriately terminated as possible to minimize these issues.

events.title: Shared Mechanobiological Programs Governing Valvular Morphogenesis, Disease Pathogenesis, and Tissue Engineering
events.description: The embryonic heart grows over 100-fold in size while transitioning from a single pulsatile tube to a 4 chambered and valved structure sufficient for sustaining one-way blood flow for over 2.5 billion beats in a lifetime. events.title: Systematically Controlled Catalysis of Non-Thermal Ammonia Reactions in Plasma-Assisted and Electrochemical Systems
events.description: Electrified non-thermal catalytic reactors can exploit green energy to synthesize chemicals and fuels, bypassing their typically large carbon footprint. One chemical with a large market is ammonia (currently for fertilizers and in the future for hydrogen carriers); however, its production via emerging non-thermal plasma (NTP) and electrochemical reactors suffers from low energy efficiencies. Elucidating the effect of catalyst material properties and reaction dynamics on these complex non-thermal systems aids in identifying and designing promising catalysts for higher efficiencies.

events.title: Mechanical Microenvironment and Signaling Dynamics in Lung Epithelial Culture Models
events.description: The lung is a vital organ with diverse epithelial cells performing specialized functions in the airway and alveolar regions. These cells reside within a microenvironment defined by a unique combination of extracellular matrix components, mechanical properties, and lung-resident stromal cells, all of which collectively guide epithelial behavior. In richer settings, however, the problem extends beyond a single agent and fixed environment: intelligent behavior may require strategic interaction with other agents, adaptation to uncertainty, and the ability to reason over complex, high-dimensional worlds. Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond have emerged as versatile quantum sensors, offering atomic-scale spatial resolution and high sensitivity. For many sensing applications, interaction with external targets requires positioning NV centers within nanometers of the diamond surface. However, the optical and spin properties of NV centers are often degraded near the surface, limiting their sensing performance. events.title: Tunable High-Throughput On-Chip Size-Based Particle Sorting Systems
events.description: Conventional deterministic lateral displacement (DLD) devices are widely used for continuous, high-resolution, size-based separation of microparticles using a tilted array of periodically arranged microposts. However, conventional DLD devices lack tunability of the critical particle size for sorting, which is fixed by the device geometry. In addition, many rows and columns of microposts are required to achieve sufficient spatial separation between large and small particles after lateral displacement, resulting in a large device footprint and reduced throughput per unit area. events.title: Design Methods for High Frequency High Performance Power Inverters

events.description: Bioelectricity plays a critical role beyond muscles and the nervous system, responsible for modulating morphogenesis, immune response, and wound healing across species. One key mechanism is electrotaxis: directed cell migration under an electric field. However, unlike action potentials, these electric cues operate under direct current (DC), creating electrochemical challenges that limit the exploration of this powerful modality. events.title: Optimized designs and performance limits for linear and nonlinear photonics
events.description: Advances in computing power and optimization algorithms have transformed photonics engineering. Whereas device designs once relied on a small set of intuitive geometric motifs—such as bowtie antennas, photonic crystals, or ring resonators—with only a few tunable parameters, modern inverse design can treat essentially every computational pixel as a design variable. This shift enables dramatic performance gains and often yields highly non-intuitive structures, raising a natural question: what are the fundamental limits on device performance, and how close are current designs to those limits?

Progress toward scalable quantum computing requires advances across multiple layers of the hardware and control stack, including qubit design, the implementation of high-fidelity interactions, and the development of experiments involving many qubits. This study uses semiconductor quantum dots (QD) in CRISPR technology (1) as a color code for multiplexed detection and (2) as a reporter for field-deployable detection. events.description: When a strong perpendicular magnetic field is applied to a two-dimensional electron system at low temperatures, electrons occupy quantized Landau levels (LLs) and Coulomb interactions dominate, giving rise to a variety of many-body ground states. events.description: Photonic hyperuniform structures are metamaterials that exhibit a hybrid state of matter by blending crystal and liquid-like characteristics. Characterizing these novel heterostructures using point statistics reveals long range order in another-wise inherently disordered medium. Photonic hyperuniform structures show photonic band-gaps, analogous to photonic crystals, while avoiding the spatial anisotropy so often hindering the utility of the ordered structures. events.title: Hardware Mechanisms for AI Capability Control
events.description: As AI capability scales faster than our ability to interpret its internals, frontier models have begun exhibiting unintended behaviors, motivating the need for robust safety mechanisms. Existing software guardrails share a fundamental weakness: they operate within the same stack as the models they constrain, making them vulnerable to a sufficiently capable AI. Hardware, by contrast, imposes physical limits on available resources that directly determine AI capability, which no application can modify or bypass.This talk presents a layered framework for hardware-enforced AI safeguards

events.description: For decades, computer architects can count on the promise of Moore’s Law. Exponential scaling creates opportunities instead of posing major constraints. Today, while the demand for performance continues to increase, technology scaling is failing to keep up. In the first part of this talk, we quantify this very change of scaling reality and show its impact on performance, power, area, and cost. events.description: Public discussion of AI and labor often centers on future job loss, obscuring how AI and associated algorithmic systems are transforming work today. Broadening Relationships
events.description: From birth to death, relationships are pervasive and foundational to a wide range of human experiences. They shape identity, influence health and well-being, guide social and political life, and structure inequality. The goal of this meeting is to bring together an interdisciplinary group of psychologists whose work examines relationships in new and exciting ways. This two-day event will explore three major themes: understudied topics and groups in relationship research, interdisciplinary perspectives on relationships, and novel methods in the study of relationships.

events.title: The Tory’s Wife: A Woman and Her Family in Revolutionary America
events.description: The Spurgin family of North Carolina experienced the cataclysm of the American Revolution in the most dramatic ways—and from different sides. This engrossing book tells the story of Jane Welborn Spurgin, a patriot who welcomed General Nathanael Greene to her home and aided Continental forces while her loyalist husband was fighting for the king as an officer in the Tory militia. events.title: Bibliotactics: Libraries and the Colonial Public in Vietnam
events.description: Libraries in French colonial Vietnam functioned as symbols of Western modernity and infrastructures of colonial knowledge. Yet Vietnamese readers pursued alternative uses of the library that exceeded imperial intentions. Bibliotactics examines the Hanoi and Saigon state libraries in colonial and postcolonial Vietnam, uncovering the emergence of a colonial public comprising bureaucrats, library personnel, journalists, and everyday readers who reimagined the political meaning and social space of the library through public critique and everyday practice. events.title: Loren Frank, Ph.D.

Thoughts make it possible to simulate possible futures and alternative pasts, and to use those simulations to inform choices. Here we focus on two key features of thoughts. events.description: 2026 marks the 250th anniversary of the publication of Adam Smith’s Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations, widely considered the founding text of modern economics and the most important work in social science to come out of the Enlightenment. Smith was, in addition, a moral and political philosopher who had written a Theory of Moral Sentiments and given an influential series of lectures on law before he ventured into economics. events.title: Conference Keynote Address: “250 years of The Wealth of Nations at Princeton”
events.description: For over two hundred years, Americans have claimed, contested, and reinvented Adam Smith’s ideas and reputation. How did Princetonians participate in this long historical process? From John Witherspoon’s lectures on moral philosophy to Jacob Viner’s postwar career, Princeton offers a glimpse into how universities shaped the American reception of Smith’s image and ideas. events.title: Revenge for the Sixties: Sam Alito and the Triumph of the Conservative Legal Movement
events.description: Justice Samuel Alito is the most emblematic figure of the conservative legal movement. His career, more than that of any predecessor, was boosted at every juncture by a cadre of like-minded supporters. Being conservative was his most powerful credential. His values, like those of many of his current Supreme Court colleagues, emerged from a combination of family, faith, and fundamental disagreement with the social changes of the 1960s. events.description: Fairly allocating scarce medical interventions like transplantable organs and ICU beds is a recurrent challenge. events.description: The talk will discuss how American Jews and their allies have used the categories of U.S. law to cabin antisemitism and secure a measure of safety. Nikhil Pimpalkhare will present his FPO "Predictable Inter-Procedural Program Analysis via Almost-Commuting Transition Systems"
events.description: Nikhil Pimpalkhare will present his FPO "Predictable Inter-Procedural Program Analysis via Almost-Commuting Transition Systems" onThursday,6/4/2026at 1pm in the Common Area of 194 Nassau Street.The members of his committee are as follows:Readers: Professors Zachary Kincaid (adviser), Thomas RepsExaminers: Aarti Gupta, and Mae Milano, Zachary KincaidAll are welcome to attend.Abstract follows below.Program analysis aims to automatically compute properties of programs from their source code. Since the problems that arise are frequently undecidable, the field has largely focused on developing sound analyses that over-approximate the true properties of a program. Modern program analyzers leverage complex heuristics to make these over-approximations as tight as possible, but these heuristics cause brittle performance: minor updates to codebases can cause large unintended swings in analysis precision.To help tool users anticipate analysis outcomes, a recent line of work has focused on developing analyses that are robust, and in particular \textit{monotone}: if a program is a refinement of another, its analysis should be at least as precise. Monotonicity is a meta-property of the analysis technique; it gives tool users a way to reason about how certain code changes, like refactoring a loop or deleting a variable, will affect their analysis results, without requiring knowledge of the tool's internal mechanisms. However, development on monotone program analysis had been largely confined to the intra-procedural setting (to analyzing procedures without procedure calls).This thesis develops a framework for designing monotone inter-procedural program analysis. The key idea is to connect context-free reachability results for abstract machines to program analysis via abstract interpretation: we compute the best abstraction of a program within a given model---its reflection---then exactly characterize the input-output behavior of that abstraction. We instantiate this recipe with vector addition systems with resets (VASR), a numerical computational model, establishing techniques to compute the VASR reflection of a program and the context-free reachability relation of VASR. We extend these results to Lossy VASR, a more expressive variant, producing a program analysis tool which is competitive with the state-of-the-art in verification capabilities while providing formal robustness guarantees. We further investigate Semi-Linear VASR, showing that their reachability relation can be computed in polynomial time despite exponential abstraction size. Finally, we unify the reachability results across all three classes under almost-commuting transition systems, (ACTS), algebraically characterizing properties that make these context-free reachability problems tractable. ACTS define a rich frontier of abstraction domains for monotone inter-procedural program analysis.

events.title: Tanner Lectures on Human Values with Amia Srinivasan (Oxford University) - "Psyche and the City"
events.description: Lecture I: Politics on the CouchAbstract for Professor Srinivasan's lectures:AmiaSrinivasan'slectures will explore the intersection of psychoanalysis and politics, tracing how thinkers from Freud to contemporary feminists have grappled with the potential of psychoanalysis to liberate not just individuals but societies. In her first lecture, Srinivasan will examine whether psychoanalysis can inform political discourse, reconcile justice with universal trauma, and balance structural critique with individual complexity. Her second lecture will revisit Freud’s controversial shift from seduction theory to the Oedipus Complex, questioning the implications of privileging psychic reality over objective truth. Together, the lectures will ask whether psychoanalysis can deepen our understanding of trauma, identity, and freedom in political life.Commentators:Two commentators follow each lecture (all commentators are with us both days):Cheshire Calhoun, Professor of Philosophy Emerit, Arizona State University;Dagmar Herzog, Distinguished Professor, History, Social Welfare, Women’s and Gender Studies, City University of New York;Mark Lilla, Professor of Humanities, Columbia University;Jacqueline Rose, Professor of Humanities and the London Critical Theory Summer School, Birbeck, University of London.
events.speaker: Amia Srinivasan
